content.full_text:
Re:
Farn PolrrrcAI- PRACTTcES C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
4pr1112,2002
Matt Grocott
City of San Carlos
280 Shelford Avenue
San Carlos, CA94070
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-028
Dear Mr. Grocott:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that the
Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(bX8)(A), (c)(+)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein
apply only to prospective actions.
QUESTION
To what extent are you constrained from representing clients in your capacity as a
professional building designer before boards and commissions that are either appointed
by or subject to the budgetary control of the city council, of which you are a member?
CONCLUSION
You may prepare and submit to the necessary offices or agencies drawings or
submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature on behalf of clients.
However, your contact with agency staff must be limited to responding to staff questions
and, if necessary, obtaining clarification of staff requests for revisions, and
communicating with staff regarding the movement of drawings and submissions through
the approval process. However, you may not appear before any boards or commissions
whose members are appointed by the city council or those which are subject to the
budgetary control of the city council, in representation of a client's interests. While you
1 Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Comnrission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
1 8 1 09- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
6'**i


File No. A-02-028
Page No. 2
may attend an EDCC, planning commission, or city council meeting at which one of your
client's projects is being considered, you may not in ary way assist in the presentation of
the project to the committee, commission or council by providing information or advice
to the presenter.
FACTS
You are currently a member of the city council for the City of San Carlos.
You are a professional building designer, and you operate a residential design
office doing business as Grocott Design Home & Remodel. You are a sole practitioner,
having no employees. It is typically necessary that you prepare plans, applications and
other related documents on behalf of your clients for submittal to either a planning
department andlor building department of the respective jurisdiction. The departments
then review your work for completeness and compliance with applicable building codes
and local ordinances before issuing applicable permits.
To delineate the specific permitting process in the City of San Carlos.
1) A residential design that results in a structure of less than the allowed
floor area for the site is reviewed at the staff level and then approved
or denied for submission to the building department. This process
requires contact with a staff person to review preliminary plans.
In the event a project is denied (because of neighborhood compatibility
issues), it may be appealed to the Exception Design Control
Committee ("EDCC").
2) A residential design that results in a structure of equal to or more than
the allowed floor area for the site is automatically referred to the
EDCC. The committee is composed of a planning commissioner and
two persons from the community. A non-voting staff person also
serves on the committee to provide technical advice. This process
requires contact with a staff person to submit preliminary plans and
elevations; projects are assigned a meeting date for review. Members
of the public are noticed and may attend. As a practice, you have
attended these meetings with your clients to present the design and to
answer any questions the committee may have. If a project is denied
at EDCC, it may be appealed to the planning commission. If denied
there, it may be appealed to the city council as a last resort. (In the
latter case, you have no doubtthat you would need to recuse yourself
from the action of the council.)
3) Upon frnahzatron of the planning process, completed construction
documents may be submitted to the building department for review
and issuance of applicable permits. When requested, revised drawings
are submitted to correct any errors, omissions or clarifications as
requested by the review technician. After review by the building
department is complete and applicable permits are issued, your


File No. A-02-028
Page No. 3
responsibilities, pursuant to your standard contract, are generally
complete and further contact with city staff is not required.
The EDCC is comprised of individuals from the community appointed by the city
council. The planning commissioners are also appointed by the city council.
You are currently under contract with two clients who are residents of the City of
San Carlos. One contract was signed on March 1,2001; a second was signed March 6,
2001. Furthermore, over the ten years you have been in business, you have cultivated a
healthy clientele and referral of business within the City of San Carlos; work from this
resource accounts for a substantial percentage of your income.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making or using
their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a financial
interest. (Section 87100.) As a city council member, you are a public official for
purposes of the Act.
The prohibition applies to specific activities - making, participating in making, or
attempting to influence a governmental decision. These terms are defined broadly.
(Regulations 18702.1-18702.3.) Regulation 18702.3 explains how to determine when an
official is using or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision.
"(a) With regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an official's agency or an agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control ofhis or
her agency, the official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears
before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or
contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity,
client, or customer."
Therefore, attempts to influence include appearances or contacts on behalf of a
client. However, the Act would not prohibit an official's employee or partner from
representing a client before the official's agency even where the employee or partner uses
letterhead that includes the official's name. (Novak Advice Letter, No. I-97-365.) We
realize that, in this case, you are a sole proprietor with no employees.
In addition, the Commission has recognized an exception for presentation of
architectural, engineering, or similar drawings by the official to his or her own agency


File No. A-02-028
Page No. 4
You, as a professional building designer, would fall within this exception. Pursuant to
Regulation I 87 02. a (b) :
"Notwithstanding T itle 2, California Co de of Regul ations,
section 18702.3(a), an official is not attempting to use his
or her official position to influence a governmental decision
ofan agency covered by that subsection ifthe offrcial:
'1T 1I
(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an architectural,
engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in
connection with a proceeding before any ageficy.
However, this provision applies only if the official has no
other direct oral or written contact with the agency with
regard to the client's proceeding before the agency except
for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions
prepared by the official."
This exception is limited to the preparation of technical documents. Please note
that the preparation of nontechnical documents or other materials would not fall within
this exception. (Levinger Advice Letter, No. I-88-328.) Moreover, the exception does
not permit an official to have any other direct contact with the agency regarding the
client's project except for necessary contact with agency staffconcerning the processing
or evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared by the official. "Necessary
contact" has been narrowly construed to only allow an official to respond to questions
from agency staff regarding the evaluation of drawings and submissions prepared by the
official or relating to their movement through the approval process. (Holbert Advice
Letter, No. I-90-080.) Finally, the exception would not allow the official to contact
agency staffwith respect to any drawing or submission prepared by someone other than
the official (Smith Advice Letter, No. 4-96-022.)
The basis for the exception was set forth in the staff memorandum submitted to
the Commission in connection with the exception. The memorandum stated:
"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on
the professional activities of planning commissioners and
city councilmembers who are architects and engineers.
There was general agreement that planning
commissioners/architects should not actually represent
clients before the planning commission. It was also agreed
that such architects and engineers should be able to prepare
the drawings and plans for clients, even though the
drawings and the plans would have to be submitted to the
planning commission. There seemed to be general


File No A-02-028
Page No. 5
consensus among the city attorneys that this exception is
necessary in order to allow the local architects and
engineers to continue to practice their profession when they
act as planning commissioners or councilmembers." (Staff
Memorandum to the Commission, dated April 29, 1985.)
Under the facts that you have presented, you may respond to staff questions and,
if necessary, obtain clarification of staff requests for revisions, and you may
communicate with staff regarding the movement of drawings and submissions through
the approval process. However, because the members of both the EDCC and the
planning commission are appointed by the city council and the building. department is a
city agency subject to the budgetary control of the city council, you may not appear
before any of these bodies in representation of a client's interests. While you may attend
an EDCC, planning commission, or city council meeting at which one of your client's
projects is being considered, you may not in any way assist in the presentation of the
project to the committee, commission or council by providing information or advice to
the present er . (Bucher t Advice Letter, N o. l-99 -242.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (91 6)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General CounselI
By: H
B. Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
HBA:jg

the approval process. However, you may not appear before any boards or commissions
whose members are appointed by the city council or those which are subject to the
budgetary control of the city council, in representation of a client's interests. While you

may attend an EDCC, planning commission, or city council meeting at which one of your

the project to the committee, commission or council by providing information or advice
to the presenter.

professional building designer before boards and commissions that are either appointed
by or subject to the budgetary control of the city council, of which you are a member?

CONCLUSION: You may prepare and submit to the necessary offices or agencies drawings or
submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature on behalf of clients.
However, your contact with agency staff must be limited to responding to staff questions
and, if necessary, obtaining clarification of staff requests for revisions, and

the approval process. However, you may not appear before any boards or commissions
whose members are appointed by the city council or those which are subject to the
